serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve BBQ sauce dip portion is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons), as commonly served alongside pizza crusts or as a condiment.

nutrient_source.title: Barbecue sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170143)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for barbecue sauce (generic) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent a standard commercially prepared BBQ sauce, which is the closest approved-source match for a housemade smoky BBQ sauce dip of this type.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet condiments and sauces — analogous estimate based on tomato-based sauce GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for BBQ sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous tomato-based sweet condiment data (ketchup/tomato sauce GI ~45, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). BBQ sauce shares a similar sugar-acid-tomato matrix. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 40.6 × 0.30) / 100 = 5.5 g per 30 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (867 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit BBQ sauce intake, as a single 30 g serving contributes approximately 260 mg sodium — over 10% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (32 g per 100 g) with moderate glycemic index (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use BBQ sauce sparingly; a 30 g serving delivers approximately 9.6 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~5.5, which can contribute meaningfully to postprandial glucose excursions if portions are not controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and high added sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium (867 mg/100 g) and high sugar (32 g/100 g) content of BBQ sauce warrant portion restriction, as both nutrients are associated with elevated blood pressure and adverse lipid profiles when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Sauce (also known as Barbecue Sauce or BBQ Dipping Sauce) is a thick, sweet-smoky condiment widely used in Western and American cuisine as a dipping sauce, marinade, or glaze. It is typically made from a tomato paste or ketchup base combined with vinegar, brown sugar or molasses, smoked paprika, garlic, onion, Worcestershire sauce, and a blend of spices. The result is a deep reddish-brown sauce with a complex flavour profile: tangy, sweet, smoky, and mildly spicy. In the context of this record, it is served as a housemade crust dipper alongside pizza at a Singapore-based pizza restaurant. Nutritionally, BBQ sauce is energy-dense relative to its serving size primarily due to its high sugar content (~32 g per 100 g) and moderate carbohydrate load (~40.6 g per 100 g). It is very low in fat and protein. Sodium content is notably high at 867 mg per 100 g, making it a significant contributor to daily sodium intake even in small portions. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low GI), but the high sugar concentration means glycemic load per serving is meaningful. Clinically, BBQ sauce is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (due to sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (due to sugar and carbohydrate content), and cardiovascular disease (due to combined sodium and sugar load). It is generally suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets and is dairy-free, though it may contain gluten via Worcestershire sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in BBQ sauce?
Is BBQ sauce high in sugar?

How many carbs in BBQ sauce?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy generic barbecue sauce (FDC ID 170143), which is the closest approved-source match. The specific housemade recipe at Now Pizza - Funan is not publicly documented, so exact nutrient values may differ. GI is an analogous estimate based on tomato-based sweet condiment data from Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for generic barbecue sauce; naturally zero as this is a plant-based condiment with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero as BBQ sauce contains no animal-derived fat. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero as this is a plant-based condiment. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero for a plant-based sauce with no fortification. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous tomato-based sweet condiment data; gi_category set to low per ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 40.6 × 0.30) / 100 = 5.5 g for a 30 g serving. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as exact matches for barbecue sauce. SNOMED CT code 229961007 used as exact match. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g have been replaced with actual USDA-reported values where available; no -1 values remain in this record.
